lines.translation: Les vacances d'hiver ont commencé. Ke Lin a décidé d'aller à Pékin pour étudier le chinois, tandis que Lin Xuemei voulait y faire un stage et trouver un emploi. Ils sont allés ensemble chez l'oncle de Lin Xuemei à Pékin.

L'oncle de Xuemei est avocat et sa tante est professeure d'université. Le quartier résidentiel où ils habitent offre un cadre de vie très agréable. Leur appartement comprend trois chambres, un salon, une salle à manger et deux salles de bain. Tous les meubles sont neufs et magnifiques, et chaque pièce est très propre, ce qui en fait un endroit confortable où vivre.
Aujourd'hui, c'est le réveillon du Nouvel An, la veille de la Fête du Printemps. La tante est occupée à préparer le dîner du réveillon, et l'oncle l'aide.
Ke Lin a vu un petit papier rouge affiché au mur avec un caractère chinois écrit dessus. Il a reconnu le caractère [zh:fú] qui signifie 'bonheur', mais il était affiché à l'envers.
C'est étrange, Xuemei, pourquoi ce caractère [zh:fú] est-il affiché de travers ? Le caractère [zh:fú] est à l'envers.
Il n'est pas affiché de travers. 'Le [zh:fú] est à l'envers' sonne comme 'le [zh:fú] est arrivé'. Quelle belle signification !
Je comprends, je comprends. C'est vraiment intéressant !
Xuemei, Ke Lin, venez, venez, venez, asseyez-vous vite, c'est l'heure de manger... Aucun de nous ne boit d'alcool, alors utilisons du thé à la place du vin. Levons nos tasses et bienvenue à Pékin !
À votre succès pour trouver un emploi et progresser dans vos études cette nouvelle année ! Tchin-tchin !
À la réussite professionnelle de l'oncle et de la tante ! Tchin-tchin !
À la bonne santé de l'oncle et de la tante ! Tchin-tchin !
Regardez, le Gala du Festival du Printemps a commencé à la télé ! Mangeons et regardons en même temps.
Ke Lin, en ce moment, beaucoup, beaucoup de familles chinoises sont comme nous, elles dînent le soir du réveillon tout en regardant le Gala du Festival du Printemps.
Vraiment ? Il y a un bon spectacle à la télé et de la nourriture délicieuse aussi, c'est super ! Le poisson à la vapeur que tante a préparé est si tendre et parfumé, c'est un délice.
Ke Lin, savais-tu ? Il faut absolument manger du poisson pour le dîner du réveillon, et tu ne dois pas tout manger, il faut en laisser un peu.
Pourquoi ? N'est-ce pas du gaspillage ?
Cela signifie 'chaque année il y a du poisson, chaque année il y a un surplus'. Le mot pour 'poisson' [zh:yú] a la même prononciation que 'surplus' [zh:yú], et [zh:yú] signifie 'reste'.

'Chaque année il y a du poisson, chaque année il y a un surplus', cela veut dire 'de l'argent en surplus' ? Le chinois est si intéressant. Dis, la Chine a plusieurs autres fêtes traditionnelles, et la nourriture est différente pour chacune, n'est-ce pas ?
C'est ça. La Fête des Bateaux-Dragons a lieu le cinquième jour du cinquième mois lunaire...
On mange des [zh:zongzi] !
La Fête de la Mi-Automne a lieu le quinzième jour du huitième mois...
On mange des gâteaux de lune.
La Fête de la Mi-Automne est un peu comme l'Action de grâce ; c'est une fête pour les réunions de famille. Il y a aussi la Fête des Lanternes le quinzième jour du premier mois lunaire...
On mange ce truc rond et blanc...
La Fête des Lanternes, on mange des [zh:yuanxiao] !
Oui, oui, je me souviens maintenant, des [zh:yuanxiao].
Regardez, la télé compte à rebours : 'Dix, neuf, huit, sept, six, cinq, quatre, trois, deux, un.' Il est minuit, la nouvelle année a commencé !
Oncle, tante, nous vous souhaitons une bonne année !
Bonne année à tous !
Bonne année, oncle et tante ! Je vous souhaite la prospérité !
Prospérité, prospérité, que tout le monde soit prospère. C'est le Nouvel An, oncle et tante vont vous donner des enveloppes rouges.
Merci ! Merci !... Pourquoi est-ce si animé dehors ?
Il faut faire éclater des pétards pour la Fête du Printemps. Nous en avons aussi acheté beaucoup, allons dehors les faire éclater.
Xuemei, envoie un SMS à Tianming et aux autres pour leur souhaiter une bonne année.
D'accord. Mais, je veux d'abord appeler mes parents pour leur souhaiter une bonne année, et ensuite envoyer des vœux WeChat à Tianming et Lisa.
Très bien. Oncle, tante, allons faire éclater des pétards ensemble !
Ke Lin, va avec l'oncle. Je vais tout préparer et je vous attends pour manger des raviolis.
Quoi ? On mange encore ?

language_notes.label: a
language_notes.explanation_en: Un 小区 [zh:xiǎo qū], qui signifie littéralement « petit quartier », désigne un complexe résidentiel planifié dans les zones urbaines. Ces complexes sont souvent clôturés et composés d'immeubles à plusieurs étages. Beaucoup offrent diverses installations pour le confort des résidents, comme des épiceries et même des salons de beauté. Les résidents paient des frais d'entretien. Dans les 小区 [zh:xiǎo qū] haut de gamme, on trouve souvent un club-house avec un restaurant et un centre de fitness. À Taïwan, le terme 社区 [zh:shè qū] est préféré.

language_notes.label: b
language_notes.focus_pinyin: sān shì liǎng tīng liǎng wèi
language_notes.explanation_en: 三室两厅两卫 [zh:sān shì liǎng tīng liǎng wèi] signifie « trois chambres, un salon, une salle à manger et deux salles de bain ». 三房两厅两卫 [zh:sān fáng liǎng tīng liǎng wèi] est une autre façon de dire la même chose.

language_notes.label: c
language_notes.focus_pinyin: fú dào le, fú dào le
language_notes.explanation_en: 福倒了 [zh:fú dào le], où le caractère 福 [zh:fú] est à l'envers, se prononce de la même manière que 福到了 [zh:fú dào le], qui signifie « le bonheur est arrivé ». De nombreux sinophones font attention à l'utilisation des homophones ou des quasi-homophones. Par exemple, le chiffre quatre (四 [zh:sì]) est souvent tabou car il ressemble phonétiquement à 死 [zh:sǐ], qui signifie « la mort ». Par conséquent, dans certains endroits où l'on parle chinois, il est possible de ne pas trouver de quatrième étage dans un immeuble. De même, beaucoup de gens aiment avoir le chiffre huit (八 [zh:bā]) dans leurs numéros de téléphone et de plaque d'immatriculation, car 八 [zh:bā] rime avec 发 [zh:fā], qui signifie « prospérer » ou « faire fortune ». Dans le même ordre d'idées, certains couples évitent de manger une poire (梨 [zh:lí]) ensemble, car « partager une poire » (分梨 [zh:fēn lí]) se prononce de la même manière que 分离 [zh:fēn lí], qui signifie « se séparer » ou « se quitter ».

language_notes.label: d
language_notes.explanation_en: 春节晚会 [zh:chūn jié wǎn huì], communément abrégé en 春晚 [zh:chūn wǎn], est une émission de variétés de cinq heures extrêmement populaire et très attendue, diffusée la veille du Nouvel An chinois. Elle est diffusée sur la chaîne de télévision nationale chinoise, China Central Television (CCTV).

grammar_sections.title: Adjectif/verbe + 着 [zh:zhe] + verbe
grammar_sections.explanation_en: Dans cette structure, le second groupe verbal indique la raison de la première action ou du premier état.

patterns.meaning_en: Indique que le second groupe verbal est la raison de la première action ou du premier état.

examples.hanzi: 快放寒假了, 小王正忙着准备考试。
examples.pinyin: Kuài fàng hánjià le, xiǎo wáng zhèng máng zhe zhǔnbèi kǎoshì.
examples.translation: C'est presque les vacances d'hiver. Le petit Wang est occupé à préparer ses examens. (Le petit Wang est occupé parce qu'il prépare ses examens finaux.)

examples.hanzi: 他急着去见朋友, 没吃晚饭就走了。
examples.pinyin: Tā jí zhe qù jiàn péngyǒu, méi chī wǎnfàn jiù zǒu le.
examples.translation: Il était pressé de voir un ami, alors il est parti sans dîner. (Il était pressé parce qu'il devait voir un ami.)

examples.hanzi: 妹妹哭着要哥哥陪她玩电脑游戏。
examples.pinyin: Mèimei kū zhe yào gēge péi tā wán diànnǎo yóuxì.
examples.translation: La petite sœur a fondu en larmes en demandant à son grand frère de jouer à un jeu vidéo avec elle. (La petite sœur a pleuré parce qu'elle voulait que son grand frère joue à un jeu vidéo avec elle.)

grammar_sections.title: La préposition 以 [zh:yǐ]
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 以 [zh:yǐ], qui vient du chinois classique, est utilisée en chinois moderne comme préposition. Bien qu'elle ait de nombreuses significations, 以 [zh:yǐ] peut parfois être traduite par « utiliser » ou « avec ». Dans le texte, 以茶代酒 [zh:yǐ chá dài jiǔ] signifie « utiliser du thé pour remplacer l'alcool ». Voyons d'autres exemples de cet usage.

examples.hanzi: 我们以茶代酒，举起杯来，欢迎你们来北京！
examples.pinyin: wǒmen yǐ chá dài jiǔ, jǔ qǐ bēi lai, huānyíng nǐmen lái Běijīng!
examples.translation: Utilisons le thé à la place de l'alcool, levons nos verres et souhaitons-vous la bienvenue à Pékin !

examples.hanzi: 他选课，只以兴趣做标准，不考虑将来是不是容易找工作。
examples.pinyin: Tā xuǎn kè, zhǐ yǐ xìngqù zuò biāozhǔn, bù kǎolǜ jiānglái shì bu shì róngyì zhǎo gōngzuò.
examples.translation: Il choisit ses cours en se basant uniquement sur ses intérêts. Il ne se demande pas si cela facilitera la recherche d'un emploi.

examples.hanzi: 老师以自己的生活经验教育学生。
examples.pinyin: Lǎoshī yǐ zìjǐ de shēnghuó jīngyàn jiàoyù xuéshēng.
examples.translation: Le professeur utilise son expérience de vie pour éduquer ses élèves.

grammar_sections.title: La particule 嘛 [zh:ma]
grammar_sections.explanation_en: 嘛 [zh:ma] est une particule modale souvent utilisée dans les conversations informelles pour suggérer que la raison d'une affirmation est évidente et ne nécessite pas d'autres explications.

examples.hanzi: 你不喜欢他, 不想让他天天来找你, 就告诉他嘛。
examples.pinyin: Nǐ bù xǐhuan tā, bù xiǎng ràng tā tiāntiān lái zhǎo nǐ, jiù gàosu tā ma.
examples.translation: Si tu ne l'aimes pas et que tu ne veux pas qu'il vienne te chercher tous les jours, alors pourquoi ne pas simplement le lui dire ?

examples.hanzi: 你说这个英文问题不难, 那你翻译出来给我看看嘛。
examples.pinyin: Nǐ shuō zhège Yīngwén wèntí bù nán, nà nǐ fānyì chūlái gěi wǒ kànkan ma.
examples.translation: Tu dis que cette question de français n'est pas difficile. Alors traduis-la et prouve-le-moi.

examples.hanzi: 你跟妈妈说清楚嘛, 还是选自己有兴趣的专业好些。
examples.pinyin: Nǐ gēn māmā shuō qīngchu ma, háishì xuǎn zìjǐ yǒu xìngqù de zhuānyè hǎoxiē.
examples.translation: Alors dis-le clairement à ta mère. Il vaut mieux choisir une matière qui t'intéresse.

grammar_sections.title: Le modèle (先 [zh:xiān])…再 [zh:zài]…
grammar_sections.explanation_en: Le modèle (先 [zh:xiān])…再 [zh:zài]… peut être traduit par « (d'abord)... puis... ». Comme 才 [zh:cái], 再 [zh:zài] peut relier deux propositions. Cependant, contrairement à 才 [zh:cái], 再 [zh:zài] indique que l'action décrite dans la première proposition est une condition souhaitée pour l'action de la seconde proposition. En d'autres termes, le locuteur souhaite reporter la seconde action jusqu'à ce que la première action ait eu lieu.

examples.hanzi: 我今年不想去,想等明年拿到硕士学位以后再去。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ jīnnián bùxiǎng qù, xiǎng děng míngnián ná dào shuòshì xuéwèi yǐhòu zài qù.
examples.translation: Je ne veux pas y aller cette année. J'aimerais attendre d'avoir mon master l'année prochaine.

examples.hanzi: 明年暑假我想先待在这儿打工挣点钱,然后再回纽约看父母。
examples.pinyin: Míngnián shǔjià wǒ xiǎng xiān dāi zài zhè'er dǎgōng zhèng diǎn qián, ránhòu zài huí Niǔyuē kàn fùmǔ.

hanzi: 开始放寒假了,柯林决定到北京学中文,而林雪梅想在北京实习和找工作。他们一起来到了北京雪梅的舅舅家。
雪梅的舅舅是律师,舅妈是大学教授。他们住的小区环境很好,房子是一套三室两厅两卫的公寓,家具都很新、很漂亮,每个房间都很干净,住起来很舒服。
pinyin: kāishǐ fàng hánjià le Kē Lín juédìng dào Běijīng xué Zhōngwén ér Lín Xuěméi xiǎng zài Běijīng shíxí hé zhǎo gōngzuò tāmen yìqǐ láidào le Běijīng Xuěméi de jiùjiu jiā

Xuěméi de jiùjiu shì lǜshī jiùmā shì dàxué jiàoshòu tāmen zhù de xiǎoqū huánjìng hěn hǎo fángzi shì yí tào sān shì liǎng tīng liǎng wèi de gōngyù jiājù dōu hěn xīn hěn piàoliang měi ge fángjiān dōu hěn gānjìng zhù qǐlái hěn shūfu
translation: Winter break has started. Ke Lin decided to go to Beijing to study Chinese, while Lin Xuemei wanted to intern and find a job in Beijing. They went to Lin Xuemei's uncle's house in Beijing together.

Xuemei's uncle is a lawyer, and her aunt is a university professor. The residential community they live in has a great environment. The apartment has three bedrooms, a living room, a dining room, and two bathrooms. The furniture is all new and beautiful, and every room is very clean, making it a comfortable place to live.

scenes.scene_goal: Introduce the characters and the modern, cozy setting of the uncle's home in Beijing.
scenes.scene_description_cn: 冬日，柯林和林雪梅拖着行李箱抵达北京一处高档小区。他们走进雪梅舅舅家，一个宽敞明亮、家具崭新的三室两厅公寓。舅舅和舅妈热情地迎接他们。镜头快速扫过公寓内部，展现其舒适漂亮的环境。
scenes.setting_en: The entrance and living room of a modern, clean, and beautifully furnished apartment in Beijing during winter.
scenes.camera_movement: Starts with a medium shot of Ke Lin and Lin Xuemei entering with luggage, greeted by the uncle and aunt. Then, a smooth panning shot across the living room.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Winter break. A young American man, Ke Lin, and a young Chinese woman, Lin Xuemei, arrive with luggage at a modern, upscale apartment in Beijing. They are greeted warmly at the door by Xuemei's middle-aged uncle and aunt. The camera follows them in and pans across the spacious, bright apartment with new, beautiful furniture, showing a very clean and comfortable living space. Cinematic, warm lighting.

voice_over.speaker: 旁白
voice_over.hanzi: 开始放寒假了,柯林决定到北京学中文,而林雪梅想在北京实习和找工作。他们一起来到了北京雪梅的舅舅家。雪梅的舅舅是律师,舅妈是大学教授。他们住的小区环境很好,房子是一套三室两厅两卫的公寓,家具都很新、很漂亮,每个房间都很干净,住起来很舒服。
voice_over.pinyin: kāishǐ fàng hánjià le Kē Lín juédìng dào Běijīng xué Zhōngwén ér Lín Xuěméi xiǎng zài Běijīng shíxí hé zhǎo gōngzuò tāmen yìqǐ láidào le Běijīng Xuěméi de jiùjiu jiā. Xuěméi de jiùjiu shì lǜshī jiùmā shì dàxué jiàoshòu tāmen zhù de xiǎoqū huánjìng hěn hǎo fángzi shì yí tào sān shì liǎng tīng liǎng wèi de gōngyù jiājù dōu hěn xīn hěn piàoliang měi ge fángjiān dōu hěn gānjìng zhù qǐlái hěn shūfu.
voice_over.translation: Winter break has started. Ke Lin decided to go to Beijing to study Chinese, while Lin Xuemei wanted to intern and find a job in Beijing. They went to Lin Xuemei's uncle's house in Beijing together. Xuemei's uncle is a lawyer, and her aunt is a university professor. The residential community they live in has a great environment. The apartment has three bedrooms, a living room, a dining room, and two bathrooms. The furniture is all new and beautiful, and every room is very clean, making it a comfortable place to live.

hanzi: 今天是除夕,也就是春节的前一天。舅妈正在忙着做年夜饭,舅舅在旁边帮忙。
pinyin: jīntiān shì chúxī yě jiùshì Chūnjié de qián yì tiān jiùmā zhèngzài mángzhe zuò niányèfàn jiùjiu zài pángbiān bāngmáng
translation: Today is New Year's Eve, the day before the Spring Festival. The aunt is busy making New Year's Eve dinner, and the uncle is helping on the side.

scenes.scene_goal: Set the scene for Chinese New Year's Eve by showing the festive dinner preparations.
scenes.scene_description_cn: 今天是除夕。镜头切到充满节日气氛的厨房，舅妈正忙着做年夜饭，舅舅在旁边帮忙。桌上摆着各种食材，锅里冒着热气。
scenes.setting_en: A modern kitchen, bustling with activity for Chinese New Year's Eve dinner preparation.
scenes.camera_movement: Medium shot of the couple cooking together, with a slow zoom-in on the delicious food being prepared.
scenes.video_prompt_en: It's Chinese New Year's Eve. In a modern kitchen, the aunt is happily busy cooking a large, festive dinner. The uncle is helping her. The scene is filled with warmth and the sight of delicious food being prepared. The camera slowly zooms in on a steaming dish. Festive and cozy atmosphere.

voice_over.speaker: 旁白
voice_over.hanzi: 今天是除夕,也就是春节的前一天。舅妈正在忙着做年夜饭,舅舅在旁边帮忙。
voice_over.pinyin: jīntiān shì chúxī yě jiùshì Chūnjié de qián yì tiān jiùmā zhèngzài mángzhe zuò niányèfàn jiùjiu zài pángbiān bāngmáng
voice_over.translation: Today is New Year's Eve, the day before the Spring Festival. The aunt is busy making New Year's Eve dinner, and the uncle is helping on the side.

hanzi: 柯林看见墙上贴着一张不大的红纸,纸上写着一个汉字,他认识,那是“幸福”的“福”字,可是贴倒了。
奇怪,雪梅,这个“福”字怎么贴错了,“福”字倒了。
pinyin: Kē Lín kànjiàn qiáng shang tiē zhe yì zhāng bú dà de hóngzhǐ zhǐ shang xiě zhe yí ge hànzì tā rènshi nà shì xìngfú de fú zì kěshì tiē dào le
qíguài Xuěméi zhè ge fú zì zěnme tiē cuò le fú zì dào le
translation: Ke Lin saw a small red paper posted on the wall with a Chinese character written on it. He recognized it as the character 'fú' for 'happiness,' but it was posted upside down.
Strange, Xuemei, why is this 'fú' character posted incorrectly? The 'fú' character is upside down.

scenes.scene_goal: Introduce the cultural puzzle of the upside-down '福' character through Ke Lin's curious observation.
scenes.scene_description_cn: 柯林在客厅里好奇地四处张望，他的目光被墙上一张倒贴的“福”字红纸吸引。他露出困惑的表情，指着字向林雪梅询问。
scenes.setting_en: The living room, decorated for Chinese New Year. A red paper with the character '福' is pasted upside down on the wall.
scenes.camera_movement: Camera follows Ke Lin, then a rack focus from Ke Lin's puzzled face to the upside-down '福' character on the wall.
scenes.video_prompt_en: In the living room, Ke Lin looks around curiously. He spots a small red paper on the wall. Rack focus from his confused face to the paper, which has the Chinese character '福' pasted upside down. He turns to Lin Xuemei, points at the character, and asks his question with a puzzled look.

voice_over.speaker: 旁白
voice_over.hanzi: 柯林看见墙上贴着一张不大的红纸,纸上写着一个汉字,他认识,那是“幸福”的“福”字,可是贴倒了。
voice_over.pinyin: Kē Lín kànjiàn qiáng shang tiē zhe yì zhāng bú dà de hóngzhǐ zhǐ shang xiě zhe yí ge hànzì tā rènshi nà shì xìngfú de fú zì kěshì tiē dào le
voice_over.translation: Ke Lin saw a small red paper posted on the wall with a Chinese character written on it. He recognized it as the character 'fú' for 'happiness,' but it was posted upside down.

hanzi: 没贴错。“福倒了”,“福到了”,你想意思多好啊!
pinyin: méi tiē cuò fú dào le fú dào le nǐ xiǎng yìsi duō hǎo a
wǒ dǒng le dǒng le zhēn yǒu yìsi
translation: It's not posted wrong. 'Fú is upside down' sounds like 'fú has arrived.' What a great meaning!
I get it, I get it. That's really interesting!

scenes.scene_goal: Explain the cultural pun and show Ke Lin's moment of understanding and appreciation.
scenes.scene_description_cn: 林雪梅微笑着向柯林解释“福倒了”和“福到了”的谐音。柯林听后恍然大悟，脸上露出惊喜的表情，开心地说他懂了。
scenes.setting_en: Living room, close-up on the two characters.
scenes.camera_movement: Starts on a two-shot, then a close-up on Lin Xuemei as she explains, followed by a reaction shot of Ke Lin's face lighting up with understanding.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Lin Xuemei smiles and explains to Ke Lin that 'upside down' sounds like 'has arrived' in Chinese. As she speaks, a simple, elegant animation could show the character '倒' morphing into '到'. Cut to a close-up of Ke Lin's face, his eyes widening in realization. He smiles broadly and nods, saying 'I get it, I get it, that's really interesting!'

voice_over.speaker: 林雪梅
voice_over.hanzi: 没贴错。“福倒了”,“福到了”,你想意思多好啊!
voice_over.pinyin: méi tiē cuò. “fú dào le”, “fú dào le”, nǐ xiǎng yìsi duō hǎo a!
voice_over.translation: It's not posted wrong. 'Fú is upside down' sounds like 'fú has arrived.' What a great meaning!

hanzi: 你们看,电视里在倒计时了,“十、九、八、七、六、五、四、三、二、一。”十二点了,新的一年开始了!
舅舅、舅妈,我们给你们拜年了!
舅舅、舅妈过年好!恭喜发财!
pinyin: nǐmen kàn diànshì lǐ zài dàojìshí le shí jiǔ bā qī liù wǔ sì sān èr yī shí'èr diǎn le xīn de yìnián kāishǐ le
jiùjiu jiùmā wǒmen gěi nǐmen bàinián le
jiùjiu jiùmā guònián hǎo gōngxǐ fācái
translation: Look, the TV is counting down, 'Ten, nine, eight, seven, six, five, four, three, two, one.' It's twelve o'clock, the new year has begun!
Uncle, Aunt, we wish you a Happy New Year!
Happy New Year, Uncle and Aunt! May you be prosperous!

scenes.scene_goal: 展现一家人迎接新年的兴奋时刻，以及传统的拜年祝福。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 除夕夜，一家人围坐在客厅沙发上，兴奋地看着电视里的春节联欢晚会。舅妈指着电视提醒大家倒计时开始了。随着电视里的倒计时，大家一起大声数数。零点钟声敲响，雪梅和柯林立刻向舅舅舅妈拜年，舅舅和柯林也互相祝福。
scenes.setting_en: A cozy, festively decorated living room. A large TV shows a New Year's gala. Snacks and tea are on the coffee table. The family is on the sofa.
scenes.camera_movement: Start with a medium shot of the family, quick cut to a close-up of the TV screen for the countdown, then back to the family's excited faces. Pan slightly as they turn to each other to exchange greetings.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Cinematic medium shot of a family on a sofa in a festive Chinese living room. The aunt points excitedly at the TV. Cut to a close-up of the TV screen showing a big countdown '3, 2, 1'. As the countdown finishes, they all cheer. The young woman and man turn to the older couple, bowing slightly with clasped hands, exchanging New Year greetings.

voice_over.speaker: 舅妈
voice_over.hanzi: 你们看,电视里在倒计时了,“十、九、八、七、六、五、四、三、二、一。”十二点了,新的一年开始了!
voice_over.pinyin: nǐmen kàn diànshì lǐ zài dàojìshí le shí jiǔ bā qī liù wǔ sì sān èr yī shí'èr diǎn le xīn de yìnián kāishǐ le
voice_over.translation: Look, the TV is counting down, 'Ten, nine, eight... one.' It's twelve o'clock, the new year has begun!

hanzi: 发财、发财,大家发财。过年了,舅舅、舅妈给你们红包。
谢谢!谢谢!……外边怎么这么热闹?
过春节要放鞭炮。我们也买了很多,出去放吧。
pinyin: fācái fācái dàjiā fācái guònián le jiùjiu jiùmā gěi nǐmen hóngbāo
xièxie xièxie wàibian zěnme zhème rènao
guò Chūnjié yào fàng biānpào wǒmen yě mǎi le hěn duō chūqù fàng ba
translation: Prosperity, prosperity, may everyone be prosperous. It's the New Year, Uncle and Aunt will give you red envelopes.
Thank you! Thank you!... Why is it so lively outside?
You have to set off firecrackers for the Spring Festival. We also bought a lot, let's go outside and set them off.

scenes.scene_goal: 展示发红包的传统，并通过鞭炮声将场景引向户外。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 舅妈笑着回应祝福，并给雪梅和柯林发红包。柯林接过红包道谢后，被窗外突然响起的巨大鞭炮声吸引，好奇地望向窗外。舅舅笑着解释这是春节习俗，并提议大家一起出去放鞭炮。
scenes.setting_en: The same festive living room. The sound of distant firecrackers begins to grow louder.
scenes.camera_movement: Camera focuses on the aunt as she hands out red envelopes. Then follows Ke Lin's gaze as he turns towards the window, curious about the noise outside.
scenes.video_prompt_en: The smiling aunt hands two red envelopes to the young adults. Ke Lin accepts his happily, but then his expression changes to curiosity as he hears loud firecrackers outside and looks towards the window. The uncle stands up, explaining the tradition and gesturing towards a box of firecrackers near the door.

voice_over.speaker: 舅妈
voice_over.hanzi: 发财、发财,大家发财。过年了,舅舅、舅妈给你们红包。
voice_over.pinyin: fācái fācái dàjiā fācái guònián le jiùjiu jiùmā gěi nǐmen hóngbāo
voice_over.translation: Prosperity, prosperity, may everyone be prosperous. It's the New Year, Uncle and Aunt will give you red envelopes.

hanzi: 雪梅,你给天明他们发个短信拜年吧。
好。不过,我想先给爸爸妈妈打手机拜年,再给天明、丽莎发微信拜年。
好吧。舅舅,舅妈,我们一起去放鞭炮吧!
pinyin: Xuěméi nǐ gěi Tiānmíng tāmen fā ge duǎnxìn bàinián ba
hǎo búguò wǒ xiǎng xiān gěi bàba māma dǎ shǒujī bàinián zài gěi Tiānmíng Lìshā fā wēixìn bàinián
hǎo ba jiùjiu jiùmā wǒmen yìqǐ qù fàng biānpào ba
translation: Xuemei, send a text message to Tianming and the others to wish them a Happy New Year.
Okay. But, I want to call my mom and dad first to wish them a Happy New Year, and then send WeChat greetings to Tianming and Lisa.
Alright. Uncle, Aunt, let's go set off firecrackers together!

scenes.scene_goal: 对比现代科技拜年和传统放鞭炮的活动，展现柯林的急切心情。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 在大家准备出门时，舅妈提醒雪梅用手机给朋友拜年。雪梅点点头，说她想先给父母打电话，再用微信。另一边，已经穿好外套的柯林等不及了，兴奋地催促舅舅舅妈一起去放鞭炮。
scenes.setting_en: The living room, near the entrance. Ke Lin is putting on his jacket. Xuemei is holding her smartphone.
scenes.camera_movement: Split focus: camera on Xuemei and her aunt discussing sending messages, then pans to an impatient Ke Lin by the door, eager to go outside.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Split screen effect or quick cuts: On one side, the aunt talks to Lin Xuemei, who is looking at her smartphone. On the other side, Ke Lin, already in his jacket, impatiently gestures towards the door, eager to go play with firecrackers.

voice_over.speaker: 林雪梅
voice_over.hanzi: 好。不过,我想先给爸爸妈妈打手机拜年,再给天明、丽莎发微信拜年。
voice_over.pinyin: hǎo búguò wǒ xiǎng xiān gěi bàba māma dǎ shǒujī bàinián zài gěi Tiānmíng Lìshā fā wēixìn bàinián
voice_over.translation: Okay. But, I want to call my mom and dad first, and then send WeChat greetings to Tianming and Lisa.

hanzi: 柯林,你和舅舅去吧。我准备准备,等你们回来吃饺子。
pinyin: Kē Lín nǐ hé jiùjiu qù ba wǒ zhǔnbèi zhǔnbèi děng nǐmen huílái chī jiǎozi
translation: Ke Lin, you go with Uncle. I'll get things ready and wait for you to come back to eat dumplings.

scenes.scene_goal: 以一个关于食物的幽默反应收尾，突出春节期间食物的丰盛。
scenes.scene_description_cn: 舅妈笑着让柯林和舅舅先去放鞭炮，说她要留在家里准备饺子，等他们回来吃。柯林刚从年夜饭中缓过来，听到回来还要吃东西，露出了既惊讶又饱腹的滑稽表情，摸了摸自己的肚子。
scenes.setting_en: The living room, near the door. The aunt gestures towards the kitchen.
scenes.camera_movement: Medium shot of the aunt talking to Ke Lin, then a rapid zoom or cut to a close-up of Ke Lin's comical, shocked face.
scenes.video_prompt_en: The aunt smiles, telling Ke Lin to go with the uncle and that she will prepare dumplings. Quick cut to a dramatic close-up of Ke Lin's face, his eyes wide with disbelief. He looks down and pats his stomach, comically mouthing 'Still eating?'.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's start with the first sentence. It sets the scene for our story. It says winter break has started. Ke Lin has decided to go to Beijing to study Chinese. The word for winter break is [zh:寒假]. The conjunction [zh:而] introduces a contrast, telling us what Lin Xuemei plans to do: she wants to [zh:实习], or intern, and [zh:找工作], find a job, in Beijing.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 开始放寒假了,柯林决定到北京学中文,而林雪梅想在北京实习和找工作。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Kāishǐ fàng hánjià le, Kē Lín juédìng dào Běijīng xué Zhōngwén, ér Lín Xuěméi xiǎng zài Běijīng shíxí hé zhǎo gōngzuò.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Winter break has started. Ke Lin decided to go to Beijing to study Chinese, while Lin Xuemei wanted to intern and find a job in Beijing.

At [zh:北京雪梅的舅舅家], Lin Xuemei's uncle's house in Beijing. Notice the multiple possessives are handled simply with [zh:的]. The word for uncle on the mother's side is [zh:舅舅].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 他们一起来到了北京雪梅的舅舅家。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tāmen yìqǐ láidào le Běijīng Xuěméi de jiùjiu jiā.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 雪梅的舅舅是律师,舅妈是大学教授。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xuěméi de jiùjiu shì lǜshī, jiùmā shì dàxué jiàoshòu.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Xuemei's uncle is a lawyer, and her aunt is a university professor.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to describe a living environment and apartment layout in Chinese.

narration.subtitle_en: This part describes their home. They live in a [zh:小区], which is a residential complex. The [zh:环境], or environment, is very good. The apartment itself is described as [zh:一套三室两厅两卫的公寓]. This is a very common way to describe an apartment's layout. Let's break it down: [zh:三室] is three bedrooms, [zh:两厅] is two 'halls'— a living room and a dining room, and [zh:两卫] is two bathrooms. It's a very efficient way to convey a lot of information!

on_screen_text.focus_text: 他们住的小区环境很好,房子是一套三室两厅两卫的公寓,
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Tāmen zhù de xiǎoqū huánjìng hěn hǎo, fángzi shì yí tào sān shì liǎng tīng liǎng wèi de gōngyù,
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The residential community they live in has a great environment. The apartment has three bedrooms, a living room, a dining room, and two bathrooms.

highlight_words.pinyin: sān shì liǎng tīng liǎng wèi
highlight_words.translation: three bedrooms, two living/dining rooms, two bathrooms

segments.visual_notes: Show a simple floor plan animation as '三室', '两厅', and '两卫' are mentioned, highlighting each area.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the specific terms '小区' and '三室两厅两卫' used for housing in China.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look closer at two key terms. First, [zh:小区]. This refers to planned residential developments, often gated communities with apartment buildings. It's a very common type of housing in Chinese cities. Second, the layout description: [zh:三室两厅两卫]. [zh:室] means room, specifically bedroom. [zh:厅] means hall, referring to the living and dining rooms. And [zh:卫] is short for [zh:卫生间], bathroom. So you can describe any apartment by changing the numbers, like [zh:两室一厅].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 小区 / 三室两厅两卫
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: xiǎoqū / sān shì liǎng tīng liǎng wèi
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: residential complex / three bedrooms, two living/dining rooms, two bathrooms
on_screen_text.notes: A common way to describe apartment layouts.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Planned residential developments in urban areas, often gated and composed of multistoried buildings.

highlight_words.pinyin: sān shì liǎng tīng liǎng wèi
highlight_words.explanation_en: Means 'three bedrooms, a living room, a dining room, and two bathrooms.' A standard format for describing apartment size.

segments.visual_notes: Side-by-side cards, one with a picture of a modern apartment complex for '小区', the other with a blueprint-style layout for '三室两厅两卫'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn adjectives for describing a home and the 'V-起来' structure.

narration.subtitle_en: The description continues. The [zh:家具], furniture, is all new and beautiful. Every room is very [zh:干净], clean. The sentence ends with [zh:住起来很舒服]. The structure 'verb + [zh:起来]' can describe how something feels when you do the action. So, [zh:住起来] means 'living in it feels...' or 'when it comes to living here...'. And [zh:舒服] means comfortable. So, it's a very comfortable place to live.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 家具都很新、很漂亮,每个房间都很干净,住起来很舒服。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: jiājù dōu hěn xīn, hěn piàoliang, měi ge fángjiān dōu hěn gānjìng, zhù qǐlái hěn shūfu.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The furniture is all new and beautiful, and every room is very clean, making it a comfortable place to live.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the terms for Chinese New Year's Eve and the Spring Festival.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence tells us the specific day. [zh:今天是除夕]. [zh:除夕] is a very important word; it means Chinese New Year's Eve. The text clarifies this with [zh:也就是春节的前一天], which means 'which is the day before the Spring Festival'. [zh:春节] is the Spring Festival, the most important holiday in China.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 今天是除夕,也就是春节的前一天。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Jīntiān shì Chúxī, yě jiùshì Chūnjié de qián yī tiān.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Today is New Year's Eve, the day before the Spring Festival.

segments.visual_notes: Show a calendar page flipping from the day before Spring Festival to Spring Festival, highlighting '除夕' and '春节'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand what happens on New Year's Eve and learn the structure '忙着'.

narration.subtitle_en: So what are they doing on New Year's Eve? The aunt, [zh:舅妈], is busy making the New Year's Eve dinner, or [zh:年夜饭]. The phrase [zh:忙着做] means 'busy doing'. This is a useful pattern: you can be busy with anything, like [zh:忙着学习] 'busy studying'. Meanwhile, the uncle, [zh:舅舅], is helping on the side, [zh:在旁边帮忙].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 舅妈正在忙着做年夜饭,舅舅在旁边帮忙。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Jiùmā zhèngzài mángzhe zuò niányèfàn, jiùjiu zài pángbiān bāngmáng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The aunt is busy making New Year's Eve dinner, and the uncle is helping on the side.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a cooking pot with steam rising to represent '年夜饭'. Highlight the structure '忙着 + Verb'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the description of the '福' character decoration.

narration.subtitle_en: Here, Ke Lin notices something. He sees a small red paper, [zh:红纸], posted on the wall, [zh:墙]. The verb for posting or pasting is [zh:贴]. The sentence says [zh:贴着], indicating the state of being posted. On the paper, [zh:纸上], there is a Chinese character written, [zh:写着一个汉字]. The particle [zh:着] is used again to describe the continuous state of the character being on the paper.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 柯林看见墙上贴着一张不大的红纸,纸上写着一个汉字,
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Kē Lín kànjiàn qiáng shang tiēzhe yī zhāng bù dà de hóngzhǐ, zhǐ shang xiězhe yī ge hànzì,
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Ke Lin saw a small red paper posted on the wall with a Chinese character written on it.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a piece of red paper being placed on a wall. Zoom in on the paper to show a character appearing.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand what Ke Lin noticed about the character.

narration.subtitle_en: The sentence continues. Ke Lin recognizes the character, [zh:他认识]. It's the character [zh:福] from the word [zh:幸福], which means happiness or good fortune. But, he notices a problem. [zh:可是贴倒了]. [zh:可是] means 'but'. And [zh:贴倒了] means 'it's pasted upside down'. The word [zh:倒] means upside down or reversed. This is the key observation that leads to his question.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 他认识,那是“幸福”的“福”字,可是贴倒了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: tā rènshi, nà shì “xìngfú” de “fú” zì, kěshì tiē dào le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: He recognized it as the character 'fú' for 'happiness,' but it was posted upside down.

segments.visual_notes: Show the character '福' on screen, then animate it flipping upside down as '贴倒了' is explained.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express confusion and ask 'why' something is wrong.

narration.subtitle_en: Ke Lin is confused, so he asks a question. He starts with [zh:奇怪], which means 'strange' or 'weird'. He asks, [zh:这个“福”字怎么贴错了]. This is a common way to ask why something is wrong or how it ended up in a mistaken state. He then states the problem again: [zh:福字倒了], 'The character 'Fu' is upside down.' He thinks it's a mistake.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 奇怪,雪梅,这个“福”字怎么贴错了,“福”字倒了。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Qíguài, Xuěméi, zhège “fú” zì zěnme tiē cuò le, “fú” zì dào le.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Strange, Xuemei, why is this 'fú' character posted incorrectly? The 'fú' character is upside down.

segments.visual_notes: A large question mark appears next to Ke Lin's animated character. The upside-down '福' character is highlighted.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the cultural and linguistic pun behind posting '福' upside down.

narration.subtitle_en: Here comes the explanation! Xuemei says, [zh:没贴错], 'It's not posted wrong.' Why? Because [zh:福倒了], 'the character Fu is upside down,' sounds exactly the same as [zh:福到了], 'good fortune has arrived.' The character for 'upside down', [zh:倒], and the character for 'to arrive', [zh:到], are both pronounced 'dào'. So, by posting the character upside down, people are making a clever pun to invite good fortune into their homes. What a great meaning, [zh:意思多好啊]!

on_screen_text.focus_text: 没贴错。“福倒了”,“福到了”,你想意思多好啊!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Méi tiē cuò. “Fú dào le”, “fú dào le”, nǐ xiǎng yìsi duō hǎo a!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: It's not posted wrong. 'Fú is upside down' sounds like 'fú has arrived.' What a great meaning!
on_screen_text.notes: 倒 (dào, upside down) and 到 (dào, to arrive) are homophones.

segments.visual_notes: Show '福倒了' and '福到了' side-by-side. Highlight '倒' and '到' and show their identical pinyin 'dào'. Animate a 'lightbulb' moment.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the cultural significance of homophones in Chinese traditions and daily life.

narration.subtitle_en: This use of homophones, or words that sound the same, is very common in Chinese culture. The pun with [zh:福倒了] and [zh:福到了] is a classic example. People also pay attention to numbers. The number four, [zh:四], sounds like the word for death, [zh:死], so it's often considered unlucky. On the other hand, the number eight, [zh:八], sounds similar to [zh:发], which means 'to prosper', making it a very lucky number. This is a fascinating aspect of the language and culture.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 福倒了 (fú dào le) ≈ 福到了 (fú dào le)
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Homophones (words that sound alike) carry significant meaning in Chinese culture.
on_screen_text.notes: Correct: 贴“福”字 (Paste the 'Fu' character). Correct: 倒着贴 (Paste it upside down for good luck).

highlight_words.explanation_en: Sounds the same as 'fortune has arrived'.
The intended auspicious meaning.
Sounds like 死 (sǐ), 'death', so it is often considered unlucky.
Sounds like 发 (fā), 'to prosper', so it is considered very lucky.

segments.visual_notes: Use a 'thumbs up' icon for lucky puns (福, 八) and a 'thumbs down' or 'avoid' icon for unlucky ones (四).

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express understanding and appreciation.

narration.subtitle_en: Ke Lin understands now! He says, [zh:我懂了, 懂了]. [zh:懂] means 'to understand', so [zh:懂了] means 'I get it' or 'I understood'. Repeating it shows his excitement and that the understanding has just dawned on him. He finishes with [zh:真有意思], which means 'That's really interesting!'. It's a great phrase to show you appreciate learning something new.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我懂了,懂了,真有意思!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wǒ dǒng le, dǒng le, zhēn yǒu yìsi!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I get it, I get it. That's really interesting!

segments.visual_notes: Animate a lightbulb turning on above Ke Lin's head.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn common phrases a host uses to invite guests to eat.

narration.subtitle_en: Now the uncle calls everyone to the table. He repeats [zh:来] three times for enthusiasm, like 'Come on, come on, come on!'. Then he says [zh:快坐下], 'sit down quickly', and [zh:吃饭了], 'it's time to eat!'. The particle [zh:了] here indicates a change of state—the time for eating has begun. These are very typical and warm phrases you'd hear from a host in China.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 雪梅,柯林,来、来、来,快坐下,吃饭了……
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xuěméi, Kē Lín, lái, lái, lái, kuài zuòxia, chīfàn le…
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Xuemei, Ke Lin, come, come, come, sit down quickly, it's time to eat...

segments.visual_notes: Animate characters moving towards a dinner table laden with food.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the cultural practice of '以茶代酒' (using tea for a toast).

narration.subtitle_en: The uncle notes that nobody is drinking alcohol, [zh:大家都不喝酒]. So, he suggests a toast with tea instead. The phrase for this is [zh:以茶代酒]. [zh:以] means 'to use', [zh:茶] is tea, [zh:代] means 'to substitute for', and [zh:酒] is alcohol. So, literally, 'use tea to substitute for alcohol'. He then says [zh:举起杯来], 'let's raise our cups', to welcome them to Beijing.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 大家都不喝酒,来,我们以茶代酒,举起杯来,欢迎你们来北京!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: dàjiā dōu bù hē jiǔ, lái, wǒmen yǐ chá dài jiǔ, jǔqǐ bēi lái, huānyíng nǐmen lái Běijīng!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: None of us drink alcohol, so let's use tea instead of wine. Let's raise our cups and welcome you to Beijing!

highlight_words.translation: to use tea in place of alcohol (for a toast)

segments.visual_notes: Animate a wine glass transforming into a teacup. Then show several teacups being raised for a toast.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to propose a toast with the '为...干杯' structure.

narration.subtitle_en: This is the first toast. The structure is [zh:为...干杯], which means 'a toast to...'. What are they toasting to? To two things for the guests in the new year: [zh:找工作顺利], finding a job smoothly, and [zh:学习进步], making progress in studies. [zh:干杯] means 'cheers' or literally 'dry the cup'!

on_screen_text.focus_text: 为你们在新的一年里找工作顺利、学习进步干杯!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wèi nǐmen zài xīn de yī nián lǐ zhǎo gōngzuò shùnlì, xuéxí jìnbù gānbēi!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: A toast to your success in finding a job and making progress in your studies in the new year! Cheers!

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the structure '为...干杯'. Show icons for a briefcase with a checkmark ('顺利') and a rising arrow on a graph ('进步').

segments.teaching_goal: To practice the '为...干杯' structure with new vocabulary.

narration.subtitle_en: Lin Xuemei responds with a toast of her own, using the same [zh:为...干杯] pattern. This time, she toasts to the success of her uncle and aunt's careers. The word for career is [zh:事业], and success is [zh:成功]. So, [zh:为舅舅、舅妈的事业成功干杯] is 'A toast to the success of Uncle and Aunt's careers!'. It's a polite way to return the good wishes.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 为舅舅、舅妈的事业成功干杯!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wèi jiùjiu, jiùmā de shìyè chénggōng gānbēi!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: A toast to the success of Uncle and Aunt's careers! Cheers!

segments.visual_notes: Show the '为...干杯' structure again, this time with icons for a trophy or a rising stock chart to represent '事业成功'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to propose a toast for someone's health in Chinese.

narration.subtitle_en: The scene starts with a toast. The phrase [zh:为...干杯] is a common way to say 'a toast to...'. Here, it's [zh:为舅舅、舅妈的身体健康干杯]. The word [zh:身体] means 'body' and [zh:健康] means 'health', so [zh:身体健康] is a common wish for 'good health'. Finally, [zh:干杯] literally means 'dry the cup', and it's the Chinese equivalent of 'Cheers!'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 为舅舅、舅妈的身体健康干杯!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wèi jiùjiu, jiùmā de shēntǐ jiànkāng gānbēi!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: A toast to Uncle and Aunt's good health! Cheers!

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common well-wish for someone's physical well-being, literally 'body health'.
Literally means 'dry cup'. Used when making a toast.

segments.visual_notes: Animate a clinking glasses icon next to '干杯'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn about the Spring Festival Gala and how to express doing two actions at once.

narration.subtitle_en: Next, they turn on the TV. The line is [zh:看,电视上春节晚会开始了]. The key term here is [zh:春节晚会], the Spring Festival Gala. It's a huge TV event on New Year's Eve. The second part of the sentence, [zh:我们一边吃饭一边看吧], uses the structure 'yībiān... yībiān...' to describe doing two things at the same time: eating and watching. It's a very useful pattern for multitasking!

on_screen_text.focus_text: 看,电视上春节晚会开始了!我们一边吃饭一边看吧。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Kàn, diànshì shang Chūnjié Wǎnhuì kāishǐ le! Wǒmen yībiān chīfàn yībiān kàn ba.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Look, the Spring Festival Gala has started on TV! Let's eat and watch at the same time.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A hugely popular variety program broadcast on Chinese New Year's Eve, often shortened to '春晚' (Chūnwǎn).
A structure used to indicate that two actions are happening simultaneously.

segments.visual_notes: Show a TV icon with '春节晚会' on the screen. Highlight '一边' in both places to show the parallel structure.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the cultural tradition of watching the Gala while having New Year's Eve dinner.

narration.subtitle_en: The aunt explains the cultural context. She says many Chinese families, [zh:像我们一样] or 'just like us', are having their New Year's Eve dinner, [zh:年夜饭], while watching the Spring Festival Gala. [zh:年夜饭] is a crucial term. 'Nián' means year, 'yè' means night, and 'fàn' means meal. Together, it's the most important family reunion dinner of the year.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 现在很多很多中国家庭都像我们一样,一边吃年夜饭,一边看春节晚会。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xiànzài hěn duō hěn duō Zhōngguó jiātíng dōu xiàng wǒmen yīyàng, yībiān chī niányèfàn, yībiān kàn Chūnjié Wǎnhuì.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Right now, many, many Chinese families are just like us, eating New Year's Eve dinner while watching the Spring Festival Gala.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to the family unit. '家' means home and '庭' means courtyard, together painting a picture of a household.
The family reunion dinner on Chinese New Year's Eve. It's a very important tradition.

segments.visual_notes: Show an animation of a family around a table with a TV in the background.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to describe delicious food with multiple positive attributes.

narration.subtitle_en: Ke Lin expresses his delight. He says [zh:太棒了], 'that's great!'. Then he praises the steamed fish, or [zh:清蒸鱼], made by his aunt. He uses a great pattern: [zh:又嫩又香]. 'Yòu... yòu...' means 'both... and...'. [zh:嫩] means tender, and [zh:香] means fragrant or aromatic. So the fish is 'both tender and fragrant'. He concludes with [zh:真好吃], which simply means 'really delicious'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 舅妈做的清蒸鱼又嫩又香,真好吃。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Jiùmā zuò de qīngzhēngyú yòu nèn yòu xiāng, zhēn hǎochī.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The steamed fish Aunt made is so tender and fragrant, it's delicious.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A classic Chinese dish. '清蒸' means to steam, and '鱼' is fish.
Used to describe the texture of food, especially meat or fish, as being soft and easy to chew.
Describes a pleasant smell, often used for delicious food.

segments.visual_notes: Animate steam rising from a plate of fish. Highlight '又', '嫩', '又', '香' in sequence.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn about the custom of not finishing the fish during New Year's Eve dinner.

narration.subtitle_en: Here comes a very interesting cultural point. The aunt asks, [zh:你知道吗], 'Did you know?'. She explains that for the New Year's Eve dinner, you [zh:一定要有鱼], 'must have fish'. But, [zh:而且不能都吃了], 'and you can't eat it all'. You [zh:要剩下一些], 'must leave some behind'. The word [zh:剩下] means 'to be left over' or 'to remain', and it's the key to understanding this tradition.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 年夜饭一定要有鱼,而且不能都吃了,要剩下一些。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Niányèfàn yīdìng yào yǒu yú, érqiě bù néng dōu chī le, yào shèngxià yīxiē.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: You must have fish for New Year's Eve dinner, and you can't eat it all, you have to leave some.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The animal and the food. A key dish in many Chinese celebrations.

highlight_words.translation: to be left over, to remain
highlight_words.explanation_en: This verb is central to the cultural custom being discussed.

segments.visual_notes: Show a fish on a plate, then animate some of it disappearing, but a portion remaining.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the word for 'wasteful' and how to form a rhetorical question.

narration.subtitle_en: Ke Lin asks a logical question: [zh:为什么], 'Why?'. He follows up with [zh:那不是浪费吗]. This is a rhetorical question meaning, 'Isn't that wasteful?'. The key word here is [zh:浪费], which means 'to waste' or 'wasteful'. It's a common concern, but as we'll see, there's a symbolic reason for not finishing the fish.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 为什么?那不是浪费吗?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Wèishéme? Nà bùshì làngfèi ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Why? Isn't that wasteful?

highlight_words.explanation_en: The standard way to ask 'why' in Chinese.
Composed of '浪' (wave) and '费' (fee/expense), it vividly means to use resources extravagantly.

segments.visual_notes: Show a question mark icon, followed by a trash can icon with a red cross over it to illustrate the concept of 'not wasting'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the symbolic meaning behind leaving fish, based on a homophone.

narration.subtitle_en: This is the punchline! The aunt explains the saying [zh:年年有鱼, 年年有余]. This means 'every year have fish, every year have surplus'. The magic is in the pronunciation. The word for fish, [zh:鱼], and the word for surplus, [zh:余], are both pronounced 'yú'. So, by having fish, you are symbolically wishing for a surplus of food, money, and luck in the coming year. The character [zh:余] has the meaning of [zh:剩下], 'to be leftover', which connects everything.

on_screen_text.focus_text: “年年有鱼,年年有余”呀。“鱼”跟“余”发音一样。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: “Niánnián yǒu yú, niánnián yǒu yú” ya. “Yú” gēn “yú” fāyīn yīyàng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: 'Every year there is fish, every year there is surplus.' 'Fish' (yú) and 'surplus' (yú) have the same pronunciation.

highlight_words.translation: have a surplus year after year
highlight_words.explanation_en: A common New Year's greeting, wishing for abundance. It's a pun on '年年有鱼' (having fish every year).
A classic example of a homophone used for auspicious meaning in Chinese culture.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the characters '鱼' and '余' appearing side-by-side with the pinyin 'yú' underneath both to emphasize they are homophones.

segments.teaching_goal: To see how the concept of 'surplus' is connected to money and how to transition to a new topic.

narration.subtitle_en: Ke Lin gets it! He connects the dots and asks if [zh:年年有余] means having [zh:剩下钱], or 'leftover money'. That's exactly the implication! He then expresses his fascination, saying [zh:中文真有意思], 'Chinese is so interesting'. He then cleverly transitions the topic by asking about other [zh:传统节日], 'traditional holidays', and their different foods. This is a great way to broaden a conversation.

on_screen_text.focus_text: “年年有余”,是“剩下钱”吗?中国还有好几个传统节日,对吧?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: “Niánnián yǒu yú”, shì “shèngxià qián” ma? Zhōngguó hái yǒu hǎojǐ ge chuántǒng jiérì, duì ba?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: 'Every year there is surplus,' does that mean 'leftover money'? China has other traditional holidays, right?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to customs, beliefs, or practices passed down through generations.
A day of celebration. '节' means festival and '日' means day.

segments.visual_notes: Show a coin icon next to '剩下钱'. Then, show a calendar icon with several dates circled to represent '传统节日'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the name and date of the Dragon Boat Festival.

narration.subtitle_en: The aunt confirms with [zh:对], 'Right.' and begins to list another festival. She mentions the [zh:端午节], or Dragon Boat Festival. She also specifies its date: [zh:农历五月初五]. This is important. [zh:农历] means the lunar calendar, which is used for most traditional Chinese holidays. [zh:五月初五] means the fifth day of the fifth month on that lunar calendar.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 对。农历五月初五端午节……
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Duì. Nónglì wǔ yuè chū wǔ Duānwǔjié...
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Right. The Dragon Boat Festival is on the fifth day of the fifth lunar month...

highlight_words.explanation_en: The traditional Chinese calendar, based on the cycles of the moon. '农' relates to agriculture.
A major traditional holiday, celebrated with dragon boat races and eating zongzi.

segments.visual_notes: Display a small calendar showing '农历' and highlight '五月' and '初五'. Show an icon of a dragon boat.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the name of the special food eaten during the Dragon Boat Festival.

narration.subtitle_en: Ke Lin excitedly finishes her sentence! He shouts [zh:吃粽子], 'Eat zongzi!'. This shows he already knows a bit about Chinese culture. [zh:粽子], or zongzi, are sticky rice dumplings wrapped in bamboo leaves. They are the signature food of the Dragon Boat Festival, just as fish is for New Year's Eve.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 吃粽子!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Chī zòngzi!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Eat zongzi!

highlight_words.explanation_en: Sticky rice dumplings, often with fillings, wrapped in bamboo or reed leaves. The traditional food for the Dragon Boat Festival.

segments.visual_notes: Show an animation of a zongzi, a pyramid-shaped dumpling, appearing on screen.

segments.teaching_goal: To introduce the Mid-Autumn Festival and how to state its date on the lunar calendar.

narration.subtitle_en: The dialogue continues with a discussion about another important holiday. The line begins with a date: [zh:八月十五]. This means the 15th day of the 8th month in the lunar calendar. This date marks the [zh:中秋节], or the Mid-Autumn Festival. The ellipses at the end suggest the speaker is about to say more about it.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 八月十五中秋节…………
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Bāyuè shíwǔ Zhōngqiūjié…………
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The Mid-Autumn Festival is on the fifteenth day of the eighth month...

highlight_words.explanation_en: A general term for any festival or holiday.

segments.visual_notes: Show a calendar highlighting the 8th month, 15th day. An icon of a full moon can appear next to the text.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach the traditional food associated with the Mid-Autumn Festival.

narration.subtitle_en: This short sentence tells us the key activity for this festival. It says [zh:吃月饼]. The verb [zh:吃] means 'to eat.' The object, [zh:月饼], is 'mooncake,' the traditional food for the Mid-Autumn Festival. So, the sentence simply means 'Eat mooncakes.' Let's take a closer look at this special food.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 吃月饼。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Chī yuèbǐng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Eat mooncakes.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A traditional Chinese pastry eaten during the Mid-Autumn Festival.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Composed of 月 (yuè, moon) and 饼 (bǐng, cake/pastry). The round shape of the mooncake symbolizes family reunion.

segments.visual_notes: An animation of a mooncake being shown, perhaps with a bite taken out of it.

segments.teaching_goal: To explain the cultural meaning of the Mid-Autumn Festival by comparing it to Thanksgiving and introducing the concept of 'tuányuán'.

narration.subtitle_en: This sentence gives a great cultural comparison. It says the Mid-Autumn Festival, [zh:中秋节], is [zh:有点像] 'a bit like' America's Thanksgiving, [zh:感恩节]. Why? Because it's a [zh:节日] 'holiday' for [zh:一家人] 'the whole family' to have a [zh:团圆] 'reunion'. The concept of family reunion is central to this festival.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 中秋节有点像美国的感恩节,是一家人团圆的节日。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Zhōngqiūjié yǒudiǎn xiàng Měiguó de Gǎn'ēnjié, shì yījiārén tuányuán de jiérì.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The Mid-Autumn Festival is a bit like Thanksgiving in America; it's a holiday for family reunions.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to the gathering of family members, especially during a festival.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 团 (tuán) means group or to gather, and 圆 (yuán) means round or complete. Together, they create a powerful image of a family coming together to be whole.

segments.visual_notes: Side-by-side icons: one for Mid-Autumn Festival (moon, lantern) and one for Thanksgiving (turkey, pumpkin). A connecting line labeled 'Family Reunion' appears between them.

segments.teaching_goal: To introduce another major festival, the Lantern Festival, and its date.

narration.subtitle_en: The speaker then introduces another holiday. The phrase [zh:还有] means 'also, there is...'. The festival is the [zh:元宵节], the Lantern Festival. Its date is [zh:正月十五], which is the 15th day of the first lunar month. This festival marks the end of the Chinese New Year celebrations.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 还有正月十五元宵节…………
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Hái yǒu zhēngyuè shíwǔ Yuánxiāojié…………
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: There's also the Lantern Festival on the fifteenth day of the first lunar month...

highlight_words.explanation_en: A festival celebrated on the 15th day of the first lunar month, marking the end of the New Year period.

segments.visual_notes: An animation of Chinese lanterns floating up into the sky.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to use descriptive adjectives to talk about an object when you can't remember its name.

narration.subtitle_en: Here, the character is trying to remember the food for the Lantern Festival. They say [zh:吃那个], 'eat that...'. Then they describe it as [zh:圆圆、白白的东西], 'that round, white thing'. Using reduplicated adjectives like [zh:圆圆] for 'round' and [zh:白白] for 'white' makes the description more vivid. [zh:东西] is a general word for 'thing'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 吃那个圆圆、白白的东西…………
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Chī nàge yuányuán, báibái de dōngxi…………
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Eat that round, white thing...

highlight_words.explanation_en: The adjective 'yuán' (round) is repeated for emphasis, a common feature in colloquial Chinese.
A common noun for 'thing' or 'stuff'.

segments.visual_notes: A thought bubble appears with a question mark inside. The words '圆圆' and '白白' pop up next to a blurry, round, white object.

segments.teaching_goal: To teach the name of the food for the Lantern Festival and introduce the modal particle 'ma'.

narration.subtitle_en: Another character chimes in with the answer. On [zh:元宵节], the Lantern Festival, you eat [zh:元宵]! The name of the food is the same as the festival, just without the character for 'festival'. The particle [zh:嘛] at the end suggests this is obvious. It's like saying, 'Well, of course you eat yuanxiao on the Lantern Festival!'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 元宵节,吃元宵嘛!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Yuánxiāojié, chī yuánxiāo ma!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: The Lantern Festival, you eat yuanxiao!
on_screen_text.notes: The particle 嘛 (ma) suggests the information is obvious.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Sweet glutinous rice balls eaten during the Lantern Festival. The name is similar to the festival itself.

segments.visual_notes: The blurry object from the previous scene sharpens to reveal a bowl of yuanxiao. The character '嘛' flashes to emphasize the 'of course' tone.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express sudden realization or memory.

narration.subtitle_en: The first speaker finally remembers. They exclaim, [zh:对了, 对了], which means 'Right, right!'. This is followed by [zh:我想起来了], 'I remember now'. This is a very useful phrase for when something clicks in your mind. Finally, they confirm the name of the food: [zh:元宵].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 对了,对了,我想起来了,元宵。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Duì le, duì le, wǒ xiǎng qǐlái le, yuánxiāo.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Right, right, I remember now, yuanxiao.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A resultative complement indicating the action of thinking (想) has resulted in recalling something (起来).

segments.visual_notes: A lightbulb icon appears above the speaker's head, and the question mark from before turns into an exclamation point.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the scene of a New Year's countdown on TV.

narration.subtitle_en: The scene shifts to New Year's Eve. A character says, [zh:你们看], 'You all, look!'. They point to the TV, [zh:电视里], where a [zh:倒计时], a 'countdown', is happening. The word [zh:倒] means 'reverse' and [zh:计时] means 'to time', so together it's a countdown. Then we hear the numbers from ten down to one.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你们看,电视里在倒计时了,“十、九、八、七、六、五、四、三、二、一。”
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Nǐmen kàn, diànshì lǐ zài dàojìshí le, “shí, jiǔ, bā, qī, liù, wǔ, sì, sān, èr, yī.”
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Look, the TV is counting down, 'Ten, nine, eight, seven, six, five, four, three, two, one.'

highlight_words.explanation_en: The act of counting backwards to a specific event.

segments.visual_notes: An animation of a TV screen showing numbers counting down from 10 to 1.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to announce the arrival of the new year.

narration.subtitle_en: The countdown finishes, and the moment arrives! The line says [zh:十二点了], 'It's twelve o'clock.' This is immediately followed by the exciting declaration: [zh:新的一年开始了], 'The new year has begun!'. [zh:新的] means 'new', [zh:一年] means 'year', and [zh:开始了] means 'has started'. It's a moment of celebration.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 十二点了,新的一年开始了!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Shí'èr diǎn le, xīn de yī nián kāishǐ le!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: It's twelve o'clock, the new year has begun!

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common verb used to indicate the beginning of an action or event.

segments.visual_notes: Animation of fireworks exploding as the clock strikes 12. The text '新的一年开始了!' appears in a celebratory font.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to give New Year's greetings to elders using the term 'bàinián'.

narration.subtitle_en: Immediately after the new year arrives, the younger people offer greetings to their elders. They address their [zh:舅舅], 'uncle', and [zh:舅妈], 'aunt'. The key phrase here is [zh:我们给你们拜年了]. The verb [zh:拜年] means to pay a formal New Year's visit or to wish someone a happy new year. This is a very important tradition during the Spring Festival.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 舅舅、舅妈,我们给你们拜年了!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Jiùjiu, jiùmā, wǒmen gěi nǐmen bàinián le!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Uncle, Aunt, we wish you a Happy New Year!

highlight_words.explanation_en: The specific term for one's maternal uncle.
The wife of one's maternal uncle.

highlight_words.translation: to pay a New Year's call
highlight_words.explanation_en: The traditional act of visiting relatives and friends to offer New Year's greetings.

segments.visual_notes: Animation of younger characters bowing respectfully to older characters, with red envelopes possibly being exchanged.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn a general New Year's greeting for a group of people.

narration.subtitle_en: This is a warm response and a greeting to the whole group. The speaker says [zh:大家过年好]. [zh:大家] means 'everyone'. [zh:过年好] is another very common way to say 'Happy New Year'. It literally means 'pass the year well'. It's a friendly and inclusive greeting suitable for any group setting during the holiday.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 大家过年好!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Dàjiā guònián hǎo!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Happy New Year, everyone!

highlight_words.explanation_en: A pronoun used to refer to everybody in a group.
A common greeting used during the Chinese New Year period.

segments.visual_notes: The text '大家过年好!' appears over a festive background with the whole group of characters smiling.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the most famous and auspicious New Year's greeting: 'Gōngxǐ fācái'.

narration.subtitle_en: Here, the characters repeat the greeting [zh:过年好] to their uncle and aunt specifically. Then, they add one of the most famous New Year's phrases: [zh:恭喜发财]. [zh:恭喜] means 'congratulations', and [zh:发财] means 'to get rich' or 'to prosper'. So, you are wishing them prosperity and good fortune in the new year. You will hear this phrase everywhere during Chinese New Year!

on_screen_text.focus_text: 舅舅、舅妈过年好!恭喜发财!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Jiùjiu, jiùmā guònián hǎo! Gōngxǐ fācái!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Happy New Year, Uncle and Aunt! May you be prosperous!

highlight_words.explanation_en: A very common and auspicious greeting during Chinese New Year, wishing someone wealth and prosperity.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: 恭喜 (gōngxǐ) means 'congratulations/joyful wishes'. 发财 (fācái) means 'to make a fortune'.

segments.visual_notes: The characters '恭喜发财' appear in a stylized, golden font, with animated gold coins falling around them.

segments.segment_title: New Year's Greetings and Red Envelopes
segments.teaching_goal: To understand common Chinese New Year greetings and the tradition of giving red envelopes.

narration.subtitle_en: The uncle starts with a very common New Year's greeting: [zh:发财、发财,大家发财]. The word [zh:发财] means 'to get rich' or 'to prosper,' and repeating it emphasizes the wish. [zh:大家] means 'everyone.' Then he says [zh:过年了], meaning 'It's the New Year now!' Finally, he announces that he and the aunt, [zh:舅舅、舅妈], will give them [zh:红包], which are red envelopes containing money, a traditional gift for good luck.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 发财、发财,大家发财。过年了,舅舅、舅妈给你们红包。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Fācái, fācái, dàjiā fācái. Guònián le, jiùjiu, jiùmā gěi nǐmen hóngbāo.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Prosperity, prosperity, may everyone be prosperous. It's the New Year, Uncle and Aunt will give you red envelopes.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A common greeting during Chinese New Year, wishing someone wealth and prosperity in the coming year.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Composed of [zh:发] (fā), meaning 'to develop' or 'to prosper,' and [zh:财] (cái), meaning 'wealth' or 'fortune'.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A red packet containing money, given as a gift during holidays or special occasions like weddings and Chinese New Year.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Literally 'red packet.' [zh:红] (hóng) is 'red,' a color symbolizing luck and happiness, and [zh:包] (bāo) is 'packet' or 'bag'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate red envelopes appearing from the uncle and aunt's hands. Show gold coins popping out of the word '发财'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to express thanks and ask about a lively situation.

narration.subtitle_en: The characters respond with [zh:谢谢!谢谢!], a simple and polite 'Thank you!'. Then, one of them notices the noise outside and asks, [zh:外边怎么这么热闹?]. [zh:外边] means 'outside.' [zh:怎么] means 'how' or 'why.' [zh:这么] means 'so' or 'this.' The key word here is [zh:热闹], which means 'lively' or 'bustling with noise and excitement.' The question asks why it's so lively outside.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 谢谢!谢谢!……外边怎么这么热闹?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xièxie! Xièxie! ... Wàibian zěnme zhème rènao?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Thank you! Thank you!... Why is it so lively outside?

highlight_words.explanation_en: Describes a place or a scene that is full of people, noise, and excitement, often in a festive or positive way.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Made of [zh:热] (rè), meaning 'hot' or 'enthusiastic,' and [zh:闹] (nào), meaning 'noisy.' Together, they create a vivid image of a bustling, energetic atmosphere.

segments.visual_notes: Show an animation of a bustling street scene with lanterns and crowds outside the window.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn about the tradition of setting off firecrackers for Spring Festival.

narration.subtitle_en: The aunt explains the noise: [zh:过春节要放鞭炮]. This means 'You have to set off firecrackers for the Spring Festival.' [zh:过春节] is 'to celebrate the Spring Festival,' and [zh:放鞭炮] is the action of 'setting off firecrackers.' She then adds that they also bought a lot, [zh:我们也买了很多], and suggests they go out and set them off: [zh:出去放吧].

on_screen_text.focus_text: 过春节要放鞭炮。我们也买了很多,出去放吧。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Guò Chūnjié yào fàng biānpào. Wǒmen yě mǎi le hěn duō, chūqù fàng ba.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: You have to set off firecrackers for the Spring Festival. We also bought a lot, let's go outside and set them off.

highlight_words.explanation_en: The most important traditional Chinese holiday, also known as Chinese New Year. It marks the beginning of the lunar new year.
A traditional custom during Spring Festival to scare away evil spirits and celebrate the new year.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: [zh:放] (fàng) means 'to set off' or 'to release.' [zh:鞭炮] (biānpào) are 'firecrackers'.

segments.visual_notes: Animate firecrackers exploding in bright colors. Highlight the characters '放鞭炮'.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to suggest sending New Year's greetings using modern technology.

narration.subtitle_en: Here, the aunt suggests a modern way to send greetings. She says to Xuemei, [zh:你给天明他们发个短信拜年吧]. Let's break this down. [zh:发个短信] means 'to send a text message.' The verb is [zh:发], 'to send,' and [zh:短信] is 'text message.' The purpose of the message is to [zh:拜年], which means 'to pay a New Year's call' or 'to wish someone a Happy New Year.' The particle [zh:吧] at the end makes it a suggestion.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 雪梅,你给天明他们发个短信拜年吧。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Xuěméi, nǐ gěi Tiānmíng tāmen fā ge duǎnxìn bàinián ba.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Xuemei, send a text message to Tianming and the others to wish them a Happy New Year.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A short message sent via mobile phone.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: Literally 'short letter.' [zh:短] (duǎn) means 'short,' and [zh:信] (xìn) means 'letter' or 'message'.

highlight_words.translation: to wish a Happy New Year
highlight_words.explanation_en: The act of visiting or contacting friends and relatives to give them good wishes for the New Year.
highlight_words.character_insight_en: [zh:拜] (bài) means 'to pay respect' or 'to visit.' [zh:年] (nián) means 'year'.

segments.visual_notes: Show a phone with a text message being typed. The message content could be a simple '新年快乐!'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to express a sequence of actions using '先...再...'.

narration.subtitle_en: Xuemei agrees with [zh:好] ('Okay'), but has her own plan. She says, [zh:不过], meaning 'but,' and then lays out a sequence. Notice the pattern [zh:先...再...], which means 'first... then...'. She wants to [zh:先给爸爸妈妈打手机拜年], 'first call mom and dad to wish them a Happy New Year,' and [zh:再给天明、丽莎发微信拜年], 'then send WeChat greetings to Tianming and Lisa.' This structure is very useful for describing the order of events.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 好。不过,我想先给爸爸妈妈打手机拜年,再给天明、丽莎发微信拜年。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Hǎo. Búguò, wǒ xiǎng xiān gěi bàba māma dǎ shǒujī bàinián, zài gěi Tiānmíng, Lìshā fā Wēixìn bàinián.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Okay. But, I want to call my mom and dad first to wish them a Happy New Year, and then send WeChat greetings to Tianming and Lisa.

highlight_words.explanation_en: A very popular multi-purpose messaging and social media app in China.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: (xiān)...zài...
grammar_points.explanation_en: Indicates a sequence of actions: '(first)... then...'. The action after '再' happens after the action with '先' is completed.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '先' and '再' in the sentence. Show two icons: a phone call icon first, followed by a WeChat icon.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to agree and make an enthusiastic suggestion.

narration.subtitle_en: Ke Lin agrees to Xuemei's plan by saying [zh:好吧], which is a casual 'Alright' or 'Okay.' Then, he turns to the uncle and aunt, [zh:舅舅, 舅妈], and enthusiastically suggests, [zh:我们一起去放鞭炮吧!]. This means, 'Let's go set off firecrackers together!' [zh:我们一起] means 'we together,' and the particle [zh:吧] again turns the statement into a friendly suggestion.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 好吧。舅舅,舅妈,我们一起去放鞭炮吧!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Hǎo ba. Jiùjiu, jiùmā, wǒmen yìqǐ qù fàng biānpào ba!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Alright. Uncle, Aunt, let's go set off firecrackers together!

segments.visual_notes: Show the characters looking excited, with an animation of firecrackers in a thought bubble above Ke Lin's head.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to delegate a task and mention a future activity, and learn about the tradition of eating dumplings.

narration.subtitle_en: The aunt responds by telling Ke Lin to go with the uncle: [zh:你和舅舅去吧]. She says she will stay behind to [zh:准备准备], which means 'to prepare' or 'get things ready.' The verb repetition softens the tone. What is she preparing for? [zh:等你们回来吃饺子] - 'Wait for you to come back to eat dumplings.' This tells us another important tradition is coming up: eating [zh:饺子]!

on_screen_text.focus_text: 柯林,你和舅舅去吧。我准备准备,等你们回来吃饺子。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Kē Lín, nǐ hé jiùjiu qù ba. Wǒ zhǔnbèi zhǔnbèi, děng nǐmen huílái chī jiǎozi.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Ke Lin, you go with Uncle. I'll get things ready and wait for you to come back to eat dumplings.

highlight_words.explanation_en: To get ready or make arrangements for something. Repeating it as 'zhǔnbèi zhǔnbèi' makes it sound more casual.
A type of Chinese dumpling, a very popular food eaten during Chinese New Year, symbolizing wealth.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the aunt in the kitchen preparing ingredients for dumplings. A plate of steaming dumplings appears at the end.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how to express surprise or disbelief using '还'.

narration.subtitle_en: Ke Lin's reaction is one of pure surprise. He exclaims, [zh:什么?], 'What?'. Then he asks, [zh:还吃?]. The key word here is [zh:还], which in this context means 'still' or 'in addition.' After what was likely a huge New Year's Eve dinner, he is shocked to hear there is still more eating to come. His short question, 'Still eating?', perfectly captures his disbelief and fullness.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 什么?还吃?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Shénme? Hái chī?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: What? Still eating?

highlight_words.explanation_en: An adverb used to indicate that an action or state is continuing, or that there is something more.

segments.visual_notes: Show Ke Lin with wide eyes and a hand on his full stomach. Animate a question mark and a dumpling popping up next to his head.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand how the 'V + 着 + V' structure indicates that the second action is the reason for the first.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's look at our first grammar point. The structure 'Adjective or Verb + [zh:着] + Verb' is used when the second action is the reason for the first state or action. In the dialogue, we heard [zh:舅妈正在忙着做年夜饭]. This means the aunt is busy *because* she is making New Year's Eve dinner. The verb [zh:忙], 'to be busy', is followed by [zh:着] and then the reason, [zh:做年夜饭], 'making the dinner'. Another example is [zh:他急着去见朋友], 'He was in a rush to see a friend.' He was in a rush *because* he was going to see a friend. This is a useful pattern to explain the 'why' behind an action or state.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 舅妈正在忙着做年夜饭。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: jiùmā zhèngzài mángzhe zuò niányèfàn.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Aunt is busy making New Year's Eve dinner.

grammar_points.pattern: Adjective/Verb + 着 + Verb
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: zhe
grammar_points.explanation_en: The second verb phrase explains the reason for the first action or state.

grammar_points.pattern: 他急着去见朋友, 没吃晚饭就走了。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā jí zhe qù jiàn péngyǒu, méi chī wǎnfàn jiù zǒu le.
grammar_points.explanation_en: He was in a rush to see a friend, so he left without having dinner.

grammar_points.pattern: 妹妹哭着要哥哥陪她玩电脑游戏。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Mèimei kū zhe yào gēge péi tā wán diànnǎo yóuxì.
grammar_points.explanation_en: The younger sister cried because she wanted her older brother to play a computer game with her.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the structure '忙着' + '做年夜饭', showing the second part as the cause of the first.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn how to use the formal preposition '以' to mean 'with' or 'to use'.

narration.subtitle_en: Next, let's discuss the preposition [zh:以]. This is a slightly more formal word that often means 'to use' or 'with'. In the text, the aunt says [zh:我们以茶代酒]. This literally means 'we use tea to substitute for alcohol.' It's a common phrase when people toast with a non-alcoholic beverage. Let's look at another example: [zh:老师以自己的生活经验教育学生]. Here, [zh:以] means the teacher *uses* their own life experience to educate students. Think of [zh:以] as a way to introduce the tool, method, or standard for an action.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我们以茶代酒, 举起杯来。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒmen yǐ chá dài jiǔ, jǔqǐ bēi lái.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Let's use tea instead of wine and raise our glasses.

grammar_points.pattern: 以 A 代 B
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: yǐ A dài B
grammar_points.explanation_en: To use A to substitute for B.

grammar_points.pattern: 老师以自己的生活经验教育学生。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Lǎoshī yǐ zìjǐ de shēnghuó jīngyàn jiàoyù xuéshēng.
grammar_points.explanation_en: The teacher uses his/her life experience to educate students.

grammar_points.pattern: 他选课, 只以兴趣做标准。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Tā xuǎn kè, zhǐ yǐ xìngqù zuò biāozhǔn.
grammar_points.explanation_en: He chooses his classes with his interests as his only criteria.

segments.visual_notes: Show a teacup icon replacing a wine glass icon to visualize '以茶代酒'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand the modal particle '嘛' for stating the obvious.

narration.subtitle_en: Now for the particle [zh:嘛]. This is a modal particle used in casual speech. It suggests that what you're saying is obvious and shouldn't need explanation. When Lin Xuemei says [zh:元宵节,吃元宵嘛!], she's saying 'It's the Lantern Festival, so *of course* we eat yuanxiao!' The [zh:嘛] adds a tone of 'Isn't it obvious?'. It's like saying 'duh!' in a friendly way. For example, if a friend asks why you're carrying an umbrella on a rainy day, you could say [zh:下雨了嘛], 'It's raining, obviously!'. It softens the statement and implies a shared understanding.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 元宵节, 吃元宵嘛!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: yuánxiāojié, chī yuánxiāo ma!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: It's the Lantern Festival, so of course we eat yuanxiao!

grammar_points.pattern: [Obvious Reason], [Action]嘛!
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: ma
grammar_points.explanation_en: The particle '嘛' indicates that the reason for something is obvious.

grammar_points.pattern: 你不喜欢他, 就告诉他嘛。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Nǐ bù xǐhuan tā, jiù gàosu tā ma.
grammar_points.explanation_en: If you don't like him, then just tell him (it's that simple).

segments.visual_notes: Use a lightbulb or a 'ding!' animation when '嘛' appears to emphasize the 'obvious' nature.

segments.teaching_goal: To master the '(先)...再...' pattern to express a sequence of actions.

narration.subtitle_en: Our final grammar point is the pattern [zh:先]...[zh:再]..., which means 'first... then...'. It's used to describe a sequence of actions. The word [zh:先] is optional but makes the sequence clearer. In the dialogue, Lin Xuemei says she wants to [zh:先给爸爸妈妈打手机拜年, 再给天明、丽莎发微信拜年]. She is clearly stating the order: first, call her parents; then, send WeChat messages to her friends. This pattern emphasizes that the second action happens only after the first one is completed. For example: [zh:我们应该先好好儿讨论讨论再做决定], 'We should first discuss it thoroughly, then make a decision.'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我想先给爸爸妈妈打手机拜年, 再给天明、丽莎发微信拜年。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ xiǎng xiān gěi bàba māma dǎ shǒujī bàinián, zài gěi Tiānmíng, Lìshā fā wēixìn bàinián.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I want to first call my parents to wish them a happy new year, and then send WeChat greetings to Tianming and Lisa.

grammar_points.pattern: (先) + Action 1, 再 + Action 2
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: (xiān), zài
grammar_points.explanation_en: Indicates a sequence: 'First do Action 1, then do Action 2'.

grammar_points.pattern: 这个问题我们应该先好好儿讨论讨论再做决定。
grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: Zhège wèntí wǒmen yīnggāi xiān hǎohāo'er tǎolùn tǎolùn zài zuò juédìng.
grammar_points.explanation_en: We should discuss this question thoroughly before we make a decision.

segments.visual_notes: Use numbered steps (1. 先... 2. 再...) to visually represent the sequence.
